short: Interface and system choices that benefit the provider by steering, deceiving, or coercing users against their own interest.
body: Coined by UX practitioner Harry Brignull in 2010 (a foundational, non-DOI source), 'dark patterns' name design choices whose predictable effect transfers value from the user to the provider. The research community and regulators increasingly prefer 'deceptive design' / 'deceptive patterns' to avoid the connotations of 'dark' and to align with legal language. There is no single agreed definition; the field is better understood as a family of normative concerns drawn from psychology, economics, ethics, and law.

short: The contemporary umbrella term for asymmetric, user-adverse design — broader than literal deception.
body: Now codified in instruments such as the EU Digital Services Act, 'deceptive design' is often used as the umbrella for the whole phenomenon. Strictly, deception is only one mechanism: a design can be manipulative, coercive, or exploitative without inducing a false belief. For precision this library names the specific mode wherever possible.

short: Excessive friction deliberately placed in the user's protective path — the opposite of a helpful nudge.
body: Sludge is friction engineered to discourage choices that protect the user — cancelling, refunding, opting out, or closing an account. Where a nudge eases a beneficial action, sludge obstructs one. It is the mechanism behind 'roach motel' designs that are easy to enter and hard to leave.

short: Any deliberate impediment to action; harmful or protective depending on whose interest it serves.
body: Friction is neutral in itself: a confirmation step that prevents accidental spending is protective friction, while a buried cancellation flow is adversarial friction (sludge). Asymmetry is the tell — protective choices made hard while provider-favouring choices are made effortless.

short: Valid agreement requires the player to perceive the mechanism, be competent, and have a real exit.
body: Opting into a magic show licenses deception within that frame — but consent is undermined when the mechanism is covert, the user is not competent to consent (e.g., children), the 'consent' was itself produced by a dark pattern, or exit is blocked. Consent obtained under aggressive dark patterns can be legally voidable, and awareness does not equal resistance.

short: Systems designed to bring players back and keep them playing; benign or harmful by dose and intent.
body: Streaks, timers, daily rewards, and appointment mechanics are ordinary engagement tools that industry treats as standard craft. They become harmful chiefly when combined with monetization, covert operation, or vulnerable targets — a clear case where context, not the mechanic, decides harm.

short: Influence that appeals to rational agency; the legitimate sibling of manipulation.
body: Persuasion appeals to a person's reasons and evidence and is generally legitimate. It crosses into manipulation when it bypasses or subverts rational agency — exploiting bias, emotion, or hidden influence. UX practitioners report this line is genuinely blurred in practice and shaped by commercial pressure.

short: Influencing behaviour by bypassing or subverting rational agency.
body: Manipulation works on the decision process itself — through cognitive bias, emotion, or hidden influence — in ways the person would not endorse if they saw the mechanism. It differs from deception (which targets beliefs) and coercion (which targets the choice set).

short: Removing, withholding, or penalising acceptable alternatives.
body: Coercion shrinks the choice set rather than the player's beliefs. Non-transparent choice architecture deployed without consent — and the withholding of resources needed to progress — can itself be coercive, which maps onto engineered grind, energy gating, and obstructed exits. The structural counter-move is genuine consent and opt-out by design.

short: Taking unfair advantage of an asymmetry or vulnerability — even when the user is not deceived.
body: Exploitation is the heart of the games problem: the gravest harms — randomised monetization, whale-targeting, personalised pricing — are often exploitative even when fully transparent. On the autonomy theory of exploitation, this is an autonomy violation, and it usually cannot be fixed by disclosure alone.

short: Deception inside the consented fiction (fair play) versus deception on the player-as-consumer.
body: Bluffing, fog of war, and plot twists are diegetic deception — part of the consented-to 'magic circle' of play, governed by fair-play and artistic norms. A hidden auto-renewing subscription is non-diegetic: it operates on the player as a consumer and is governed by consumer-protection norms. This locus distinction is the single most useful games-specific heuristic.

short: A paid, randomised reward container with unknown contents at the point of purchase.
body: Loot boxes (and the related gacha model) are the canonical case where exploitation, not deception, drives harm: they can be fully transparent about being random yet remain the leading harm candidate, with a replicated correlation to problem gambling that is stronger in adolescents.

short: The small share of players who generate most monetization revenue, often through randomised mechanics.
body: Revenue in randomised-reward games is concentrated in a small fraction of high-spenders ('whales'). Designs that individually profile and price to maximise a specific player's spend — dynamically targeting identified at-risk spenders — are among the clearest candidates for prohibition.

short: Rewarding on an unpredictable schedule — the most persistent form of behavioural reinforcement.
body: Variable-ratio schedules deliver rewards after an unpredictable number of actions, producing high, persistent response rates — the same mechanism that makes slot machines compelling. Combined with near-miss framing, it is the engine behind randomised-reward harm.

short: Vulnerability as a universal, situational property of the choice architecture — not just a few 'vulnerable groups'.
body: Digital vulnerability is architectural, relational, and data-driven: everyone is situationally vulnerable online, because the environment can detect and act on momentary states. This reframing explains why exploitative design can harm even competent adults, not only protected groups.

short: Designs that actively protect the user's interest — the constructive counterpart to dark patterns.
body: Fair (or 'bright') patterns are the mirror image of dark patterns: clear pricing, honest defaults, visible odds, frictionless cancellation, spend caps, and reality checks. They are the practical menu of remedies a studio can adopt as a design-review standard.

short: The migration of gambling structures and aesthetics into mainstream digital games.
body: Gamblification describes how chance-based reward, wagering aesthetics, and monetised risk have spread from gambling into ordinary games — blurring the boundary that regulation has historically relied on and raising public-health concerns.

short: Manufactured fear of missing out, used to shortcut deliberation through scarcity and urgency.
body: Limited-time banners, expiring bundles, and countdowns manufacture urgency that pressures impulsive action. As a decision-point tactic it is especially risky for minors and when offers are triggered by behavioural data at moments of heightened susceptibility.
